perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall maintain the Information in confidence and shall not disclose the information to any third party with out the consent of Lending Agent; provided, however, that Borrower may disclose the Information to the extent required by Sample 2 subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body with jurisdiction over Borrower, or as otherwise required by law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower shall maintain the Information in confidence and shall not disclose the information to any third party without the consent of Lending Agent; provided, however, that Borrower may disclose the Information at its sole discretion, regardless of subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body with jurisdiction over Borrower, or as otherwise required by law or regulation.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to allow disclosure at the Borrower's 'sole discretion' contradicts the original intent of confidentiality and undermines legal requirements for disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts FRCP 45, which governs subpoenas and requires compliance. The modified text suggests the Borrower can ignore legal subpoenas and court orders, violating the rule of law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Borrower to disclose information at its 'sole discretion, regardless of subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body.' This directly contradicts the legal obligation to comply with subpoenas and court orders, as outlined in FRCP 45. The original text provided exceptions for legally required disclosures, while the changed text removes this constraint, giving the Borrower unrestricted freedom to disclose information, even in the face of legal compulsion, undermining the rule of law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 details the requirements of a subpoena, which the modified text allows the borrower to ignore.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, with regard to its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'without regard' to 'with regard' directly alters the interpretation of which state's law applies in case of disputes. This can introduce uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules - particularly concerning contract interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts standard contract interpretation practices in New York. While parties can agree on a governing law, specifying 'with regard to its conflict of law principles' creates ambiguity as it suggests the potential application of other states' laws, conflicting with the explicit choice of New York law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The change from 'without regard' to 'with regard' introduces ambiguity regarding which state's laws govern the contract. The Law Explanation states that specifying 'with regard to its conflict of law principles' creates ambiguity and conflicts with the explicit choice of New York law. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction', as the altered text introduces an ambiguity that makes the legal obligation difficult to enforce. While the scraped snippets are not useful in this instance, the combination of the perturbation explanation and law explanation clearly demonstrates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Borrower acknowledges that Lending Agent makes no representations or warranties regarding the accuracy or completeness of the Information and that Borrower is not relying on Lending Agent to have performed any review of the Information for accuracy or completeness.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower acknowledges that Lending Agent makes no representations or warranties regarding the accuracy or completeness of the Information and that Borrower is absolutely relying on Lending Agent to have performed any review of the Information for accuracy or completeness.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is not relying' to 'is absolutely relying' completely reverses the stated understanding between the parties. This can create significant legal issues if the information is inaccurate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Contract Principles - specifically regarding Misrepresentation and Reliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change directly contradicts the principle that a party cannot claim misrepresentation if they did not rely on the other party's statements. By stating the Borrower 'is absolutely relying' even though the Lending Agent disclaims warranties, it opens the door for a misrepresentation claim, conflicting with established contract law.
perturbation.justification: The modification reverses the borrower's reliance on the lending agent's information. The original text states the borrower is 'not relying' on the lending agent, whereas the changed text states they 'are absolutely relying.' The Law Explanation indicates that this directly contradicts the principle that a party cannot claim misrepresentation if they did not rely on the other party's statements. By reversing this stance, the change introduces the possibility of a misrepresentation claim, creating a legal contradiction. The 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction arises 'when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable,' and here, the changed language creates a contradiction related to reliance and potential misrepresentation claims. Given the reversal of reliance, a core concept in contract law, and the potential for a misrepresentation claim, this contradiction is direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: In connection with any transaction entered or contemplated to be entered pursuant to the Borrowing Agreement, Lending Agent shall provide to the Borrower information regarding each Principal, including the Name, Tax or Government ID, address, and othe r specific identifying information, financial information, corporate structure and organization, and/or information concerning Loans entered into on behalf of specific Principals (collectively, the “Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with any transaction entered or contemplated to be entered pursuant to the Borrowing Agreement, Lending Agent shall provide to the Borrower information regarding each Principal, including the Name, address, and other specific identifying information. Financial information, corporate structure, and organization, and/or information concerning Loans entered into on behalf of specific Principals are optional.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to provide Tax or Government ID alters the core Information that is required, potentially preventing regulatory compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: USA PATRIOT Act - Customer Identification Program (CIP) Requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the USA PATRIOT Act's CIP requirements. Financial institutions are required to obtain a Tax or Government ID to verify the identity of customers. Making this information optional violates the Act's mandate.

perturbation.justification: The changed text makes providing Tax or Government ID optional, which contradicts the requirement of the USA PATRIOT Act's CIP requirements as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The original text mandated this information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, mentioning 'Customer identification program requirements for banks', corroborates the need for financial institutions to identify customers, which is undermined by making the Tax or Government ID optional.

perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall limit the distribution of the Information within the firm to only those credit, risk managemen t, regulatory reporting and compliance areas who perform the functions described above.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower shall limit the distribution of the Information within the firm to only those credit, risk management, regulatory reporting, compliance areas, and marketing departments who perform the functions described above.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'marketing departments' expands the distribution beyond legally necessary functions, weakening confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change potentially violates the GLBA's Safeguards Rule. While GLBA allows for certain information sharing, it mandates reasonable safeguards to protect customer information. Sharing confidential financial information with marketing departments, which may not have the same level of security or need-to-know, could be seen as a failure to implement adequate safeguards.

perturbation.justification: The addition of 'marketing departments' to the list of entities authorized to receive confidential borrower information creates a legal contradiction under the GLBA's Safeguards Rule, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the provided snippets don't explicitly detail the Safeguards Rule itself, they include references to 16 CFR Part 314, which concerns 'Standards for Safeguarding Customer Information'. Sharing information with marketing departs which 'may not have the same level of security or need-to-know' directly undermines the mandate to implement adequate safeguards and maintain confidentiality. The original text limits distribution to departments performing 'credit, risk management, regulatory reporting and compliance' functions. The changed text broadens that scope beyond these core functions, thus increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
